paper_title: ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multimodal Large Language Models have advanced visual reasoning, yet a purely textual chain of thought remains a bottleneck for questions that require fine-grained focus or view transformations. The ''think with images'' paradigm narrows this gap, but existing approaches are either constrained by fixed predefined toolkits or produce noisy intermediate images from unified multimodal methods. We pursue a third option: using a dedicated image editing model and decouple it with an understanding model. However, off-the-shelf image editors fail as reasoning assistants with two complementary gaps: a language-side gap, where editors trained as passive instruction-followers cannot map an abstract question to an appropriate visual transformation, and a generation-side gap, where edit correctness degrades as reasoning depth grows. Guided by this analysis, we introduce ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning), a question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor decoupled from the downstream understanding model and trained with a two-stage recipe targeted at the two gaps: Reasoning Imitation via supervised fine-tuning on edit trajectories, followed by Reasoning Enhancement with VLM-derived rewards for edit correctness and downstream reasoning accuracy. Since the editor is decoupled, ETCHR plugs into different open- and closed-source MLLMs in a training-free manner. Across five task families (fine-grained perception, chart understanding, logic reasoning, jigsaw restoration, and 3D understanding), ETCHR raises average Pass@1 from 55.95 to 60.77 (+4.82) with Qwen3-VL-8B, from 65.08 to 70.55 (+5.47) with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, and from 76.55 to 81.16 (+4.61) with the 1T-parameter MoE model Kimi K2.5.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 2026-5-25 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Beichen Zhang*1,2, Yuhong Liu*2,3, Jinsong Li1,2, Yuhang Zang2, Jiaqi Wang4 and Dahua Lin1,2,5 1The Chinese University of Hong Kong, 2Shanghai AI Laboratory, 3Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 4Shanghai Innovation Institute, 5CPII under InnoHK 6 2 0 2 2 2 ] . [ 1 7 9 8 3 2 . 5 0 6 2 : r Multimodal Large Language Models have advanced visual reasoning, yet purely textual chain of thought remains bottleneck for questions that require fine-grained focus or view transformations. The think with images paradigm narrows this gap, but existing approaches are either constrained by fixed predefined toolkits or produce noisy intermediate images from unified multimodal methods. We pursue third option: using dedicated image editing model and decouple it with an understanding model. However, off-the-shelf image editors fail as reasoning assistants with two complementary gaps: language-side gap, where editors trained as passive instruction-followers cannot map an abstract question to an appropriate visual transformation, and generation-side gap, where edit correctness degrades as reasoning depth grows. Guided by this analysis, we introduce ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning), question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor decoupled from the downstream understanding model and trained with two-stage recipe targeted at the two gaps: Reasoning Imitation via supervised fine-tuning on edit trajectories, followed by Reasoning Enhancement with VLM-derived rewards for edit correctness and downstream reasoning accuracy. Since the editor is decoupled, ETCHR plugs into different openand closed-source MLLMs in training-free manner. Across five task families (fine-grained perception, chart understanding, logic reasoning, jigsaw restoration, and 3D understanding), ETCHR raises average Pass@1 from 55.95 to 60.77 (+4.82) with Qwen3-VL-8B, from 65.08 to 70.55 (+5.47) with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, and from 76.55 to 81.16 (+4.61) with the 1T-parameter MoE model Kimi K2.5. 1. Introduction Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) [1, 4, 12] have substantially improved visual reasoning [20, 40], but purely textual chain-of-thought [34] remains bottleneck when question depends on where to look or how scene would change under an action. In such cases, the model must verbalize spatial state it cannot draw, and small descriptive errors compound across steps. growing line of work therefore enables MLLMs to think with images [10, 23, 44], generating an intermediate image during inference and feeding it back into the reasoning trajectory, with reported gains on fine-grained visual search [10, 38, 44], chart reasoning [8, 42], and spatial navigation [7, 15]. The difficulty of the think with images paradigm is not simply generating an image, but generating the right intermediate image for the question. The system must infer what visual change would advance the answer, render that change faithfully, and produce an intermediate image that the downstream understanding model can exploit. This couples two capabilities that are hard to satisfy simultaneously: (i) the breadth of visual transformations the system can conceive and execute, ranging from fine-grained highlight of local elements to holistic transitions in spatial perspective; and (ii) the fidelity with which those transformations are rendered, ensuring the intermediate image can indeed serve as reliable evidence for downstream reasoning. Only when both capabilities are present can think-with-image framework deliver high-quality assistance across diverse understanding tasks. Existing approaches address only one side. Tool-based methods (Fig. 1 (a)) [8, 10, 38, 42, 44] fine-tune the understanding model to emit bounding boxes, crop/zoom commands, or executable snippets that Corresponding authors: zangyuhang@pjlab.org.cn * Eqaul Contribution. Code is at https://github.com/InternLM/ETCHR ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 1: ETCHR vs. prior think with images paradigms. (a) Tool-based methods emit action tokens to renderer, limiting edits to low-level operations and requiring VLM fine-tuning. (b) Unified models share one backbone for text and images, weakening both and producing noisy intermediates. (c) ETCHR decouples question-conditioned editor from the understanding MLLM and adds verify-and-reason step, enabling plug-and-play use across tasks. (d) Across nine benchmarks, ETCHR (with Qwen3-VL-8B and Kimi K2.5 1T) surpasses tool-based and unified-model baselines. deterministic renderer applies; the actions are controllable but confined to low-level, localized manipulations, and task-specific fine-tuning can erode the models general competence [13]. Unified multimodal methods (Fig. 1 (b)) [3, 5, 7, 15, 28] instead use single backbone to interleave text and image tokens, gaining flexibility but inheriting weaker generative head: recent work [35] shows that their intermediate images often inject noise rather than guidance, while the unified backbone itself lags specialist understanding and generation models [37, 39]. Both families share further blind spot: neither verifies whether the intermediate edit is actually correct before reasoning forward from it [11, 21, 27], allowing noisy edit to propagate directly into the final answer. These limitations motivate us to pursue third option: using dedicated image-to-image editor as the intermediate-image generator. specialist editor expresses far broader transformation space than the predefined actions of tool-based methods [8, 10, 38, 42, 44], preserves the fidelity of model dedicated to editing rather than the weaker generative head of unified backbone [3, 5, 7, 15, 28], and decouples edit quality from the understanding model so the latter need not be retrained on task-specific editing formats. This option is now plausible architecturally: modern image-to-image editors (e.g., FLUX-class [14] and Qwen-ImageEdit [36]) replace the shallow CLIP-style text encoder [25] of earlier editors with an MLLM-style encoder, giving the editor enough language-side capacity to parse complex question and support cross-task visual transformations within single model. Realizing this option, however, is non-trivial. Image editors are normally trained as passive instructionfollowing tools that expect an explicit edit prompt such as add bounding box around the red car [2, 41], whereas model that thinks with images is handed only question and must itself decide what edit would help answer it. Moreover, the generative components of current editing models often lack the capacity for sophisticated reasoning, making it difficult to assist those understanding tasks requiring complex logic or 3D spatial manipulations. The central challenge is therefore to turn the editor into an autonomous question-conditioned system with strong reasoning capabilities, whose intermediate images are genuinely reasoning-useful rather than merely visually plausible. Correctness is also critical: an erroneous edit can silently mislead the downstream understanding model without verification [11, 21, 27], so the system must also be able to detect and reject its own bad edits before they enter the reasoning trajectory. We instantiate this design as ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning, (Fig. 1 (c))): questionconditioned, reasoning-aware image editor coupled with edit-verification at inference. This design uses question-conditioned editing to strengthen language-side reasoning and dedicated specialist editor to preserve generative fidelity. Unlike tool-based methods [8, 10, 38, 42, 44], ETCHR is not confined to predefined action 2 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 2: Diagnosis of two complementary gaps in image editors. (a) Language-side reasoning: editors trained as instruction-followers perform worse on abstract questions than on concrete instructions, revealing gap in mapping reasoning questions to visual transformations. (b) Generation-side reasoning: even with concrete instructions, edit correctness decreases as path length 𝐿 grows on Maze Solving, indicating difficulty executing multi-step reasoning during DiT decoding. space and infers what to change directly from the question. Unlike unified multimodal methods [3, 5, 7, 15, 28], ETCHR keeps understanding and generation in separate specialist models, so editing fidelity is not compromised by joint optimization. Going beyond both families, ETCHR incorporates reflective inference step at inference, letting the understanding model reject noisy edits rather than propagate them to the final answer. ETCHR is trained in two stages and deployed with reflective inference procedure. (1) Reasoning Imitation applies supervised fine-tuning to convert the editor from passive renderer into question-conditioned editor that infers the useful transformation from the question alone. (2) Reasoning Enhancement then uses reinforcement learning with reasoning-aware rewards, rather than only visual plausibility, to push outputs toward edits that are correct in isolation and useful for downstream reasoning. At inference, ETCHR follows an Edit-Verify-Reason procedure: the editor proposes an intermediate image, the understanding model verifies whether the proposed edit is reliable, and the answer is produced from the edited image only when verification succeeds; otherwise, the system falls back to the original image. On diverse benchmark suite covering fine-grained perception (V*Bench [38], HRBench [31]), chart understanding (ChartQA [22], CharXiv [33]), logic and path reasoning (Maze, Frozen Lake), jigsaw reasoning (built on COCO [17]), and 3D understanding (ViewSpatial [16]), ETCHR raises average Pass@1 from 55.95 to 60.77 (+4.82) with Qwen3-VL-8B [1], from 65.08 to 70.55 (+5.47) with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite and from 76.55 to 81.16 (+4.61) with Kimi K2.5, outperforming tool-based [8, 42] and unified-model [7, 15] baselines (Fig. 1 (d)). Ablations confirm that both training stages, the two reward signals (editing correctness and editing guidance), and the Edit-Verify-Reason reflection at inference, each contribute and are complementary. Our contributions: 1) We propose mechanism for thinking with images that uses dedicated image-toimage editor as the source of intermediate visual evidence, with an inference-time verification step that lets the understanding model reject unreliable edits. 2) We instantiate this mechanism as ETCHR, reasoning-aware editor built on FLUX.2-klein-base-9B [14] and trained with two-stage recipe: Reasoning Imitation, supervised fine-tuning on question-conditioned edit trajectories, followed by Reasoning Enhancement with two VLM-derived reward signals. 3) Because ETCHR conditions on the question and is decoupled from the understanding model, it pairs with different openand closed-source MLLMs without fine-tuning them, evaluated across reasoning tasks spanning fine-grained perception, spatial and path reasoning, puzzle restoration, and 3D understanding. 2. Analysis To motivate ETCHR, we decompose reasoning-aware editing into two sub-capabilities that current editors lack. Language-side reasoning infers, from an abstract question alone, what visual transformation would help answer it. Generation-side reasoning faithfully renders that transformation when it requires non-trivial spatial or algorithmic inference (e.g., tracing maze path). We diagnose both gaps with off-the-shelf editors and understanding models. Language-Side Reasoning: From Question to Edit. Modern image-to-image editors (e.g., FLUX.2-klein-base9B [14], InstructPix2Pix [2]) are optimized as instruction-following tools: they expect an explicit edit prompt 3 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning such as draw red box around the trash can rather than an abstract question such as Is the trash can on the left or right side of the black chair? We therefore probe question-to-edit gap: whether the editor can recover the useful transformation from the raw question alone. We use Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite [4] as prompt enhancer that converts each question 𝑞 into concrete editing instruction 𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑠𝑡, and Qwen3-VL-8B [1] judges correctness on 100 samples from V*Bench [38] and HRBench [31]. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the concrete-instruction condition significantly outperforms the abstractquestion condition. Shaped by explicit instruction-following, the base editor lacks reliable question-to-edit mapping; reasoning-aware editing therefore needs more than generic image-editing fidelity. Generation-Side Reasoning: Scaling Robustness to Reasoning Depth. Despite efforts to address the weak language-side reasoning with prompt enhancers, we argue that their reasoning ability is even more lacking during the DiT decoding phase. Even with concrete instruction, an editor can fail when the transformation itself requires multi-step spatial reasoning during DiT decoding. We measure edit correctness as function of task complexity on Maze Solving and Frozen Lake Solving. For these two tasks, we construct held-out set of 100 samples with shortest-path lengths 𝐿 {1, 3, 5, 7, 10} and present the shortest path in the text prompt. VLM-as-Judge marks an edit correct only if the highlighted path matches the input. Fig. 2 (b) plots edit correctness against 𝐿: accuracy is near-perfect at 𝐿=1 and decreases sharply as 𝐿 grows, approaching zero on the longest paths. This indicates that even when instructions are provided with maximum precision and granularity, current editing models still struggle to execute operations requiring multi-hop reasoning during the DiT decoding phase. Summary. Together, the two gaps motivate ETCHRs two-stage design: Stage question-conditioned imitation to close the question-to-edit gap and equip with strong reasoning capabilities, and Stage II reasoning-oriented enhancement to further enhance multi-step generation. 3. ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning ETCHR trains question-conditioned image editor in two stages. After preliminaries (Sec. 3.1), Stage (Reasoning Imitation, Sec. 3.2) converts the base editor into question-conditioned editor via supervised fine-tuning, and Stage II (Reasoning Enhancement, Sec. 3.3) aligns it with downstream reasoning utility via RL under VLM-derived rewards. At inference, Edit-Verify-Reason (Sec. 3.4) verifies each edit and reverts to the original image on failure. 3.1. Preliminaries We cast reasoning-aware editing as question-conditioned image-to-image task. Each training instance is tuple {𝑖, 𝑖𝑔𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎}, where 𝑖 is the input image, 𝑞 is natural-language question, 𝑎 is ground-truth answer, and 𝑖𝑔𝑡 is ground-truth edited image that surfaces the visual evidence needed to derive 𝑎 from 𝑖 (e.g., traced path through maze). ETCHR learns question-conditioned editor ℰ that maps (𝑖, 𝑞) to an edited image 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 = ℰ(𝑖, 𝑞) serving the same evidential role as 𝑖𝑔𝑡. Quality of 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 is judged by frozen understanding model ℳ: useful edit satisfies ℳ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞) = 𝑎 while the unaided baseline satisfies ℳ(𝑖, 𝑞) = 𝑎. This notation is reused throughout Sec. 3.2 and Sec. 3.3. 3.2. Reasoning Imitation Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) Data Preparation. We build large-scale SFT corpus of question-conditioned edit trajectories {𝑖, 𝑖𝑔𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎}, partitioned into five reasoning families spanning the visual transformations required by downstream reasoning tasks: fine-grained perception probes localization of small or easily missed referents; chart understanding probes grounding in structured plots; logic reasoning probes multi-step algorithmic inference; jigsaw reasoning probes global geometric reorganization; and 3D understanding probes viewpoint and camera-pose transformation. Our task coverage, from local annotation to whole-image rearrangement, prevents collapse onto single edit template and forces the editor to acquire meta-capability of inferring what visual transformation each question demands. For fine-grained perception, we use the 𝑉 * training dataset [38], covering both 𝑉 *-GQA and 𝑉 *-COCO subsets, and synthesize 𝑖𝑔𝑡 by rendering the annotated bounding boxes onto 𝑖. For chart understanding, we 4 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 3: Overview of ETCHR. (1) Reasoning Imitation SFT fine-tunes the base editor on curated questionedit trajectories, teaching it to infer the useful visual transformation from the question alone. (2) Reasoning Enhancement RL applies GRPO with two VLM-derived rewards, edit correctness and downstream answer accuracy, to align the editor with reasoning utility rather than visual plausibility. (3) Edit-Verify-Reason inference lets the understanding model verify each proposed edit and fall back to the original image when verification fails. draw from RefChartQA [30] and apply the same bounding-box overlay procedure to obtain 𝑖𝑔𝑡. For logic reasoning, we build an in-house maze corpus where 𝑖 shows the maze topology and 𝑖𝑔𝑡 overlays the correct traversal path. For jigsaw reasoning, we sample from Spatial-SSRL [19], taking 𝑖 as spatially shuffled image and 𝑖𝑔𝑡 as its restoration. For 3D understanding, we use DL3DV-10K [18], which contains videos of real-world 3D scenes together with per-frame camera poses. We sample 𝑖 and 𝑖𝑔𝑡 from the same video and synthesize 𝑞 and 𝑎 from the camera extrinsics. Task-level Prompt Enhancement. Using 𝑞 alone as the editing prompt causes severe cross-task interference: the editors latent space, shaped by explicit instruction-following, lacks the priors to disambiguate whether question demands localization, path-tracing, rearrangement, or viewpoint transformation. We therefore prepend task-level meta-prompt 𝑝𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘 to 𝑞, evoking the editing modality appropriate to each task family. At training, 𝑝𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘 acts as soft task-router that partitions the editors latent space into task-specific manifolds and suppresses gradient conflicts across families. At inference, it adds no architectural cost: because 𝑝𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘 is task-level, the editor needs no access to the understanding models internal representations or per-instance instructions, letting ETCHR be deployed in training-free manner atop any openor closed-source MLLMs. Training Strategy. We use Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [9] to fine-tune the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) [24] of FLUX.2-klein-base-9B [14], keeping the VAE and text encoder frozen as inputs are standard frames and prompts are simply 𝑝𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘 concatenated with 𝑞. We apply large LoRA rank (𝑟 = 768) to all linear layers in the DiT blocks to provide sufficient capacity for multi-task learning. 3.3. Reasoning Enhancement Reinforcement Learning (RL) Data Preparation. We curate the RL set by sampling 2,000 instances from each of the five families (fine-grained perception, chart understanding, logic reasoning, jigsaw, and 3D understanding) in the SFT corpus, yielding 10,000 pairs. An instance (𝑖, 𝑖𝑔𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎) is retained only if it satisfies: 𝒞(𝑖, 𝑖𝑔𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎) = 1[ℳ(𝑖, 𝑞) = 𝑎 ℳ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑔𝑡, 𝑞) = 𝑎], (1) where the notation follows Sec. 3.1. We keep only samples where (i) the understanding model fails on the raw image and (ii) succeeds when conditioned on the ground-truth edit. The first clause discards instances already solvable without visual assistance, avoiding wasted gradients on redundant edits; the second guarantees verifiable upper-bound signal per sample, yielding denser reward landscape and lower-variance policy gradients. 5 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Reward Design. Edit quality admits no direct scalar metric, so we infer it through two complementary rewards that probe distinct facets of edit adequacy and compensate for each others blind spots. Editing Guidance Reward (𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒). The first reward measures the downstream reasoning utility of 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡: it is one iff ℳ answers correctly given (𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡), 𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎) = 1[ℳ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞) = 𝑎]. (2) 𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒 is the most faithful signal, optimizing the end-to-end objective of producing edits that help ℳ answer correctly. Its fidelity, however, is bounded by ℳs capability ceiling: on easy questions, ℳ may succeed with partially erroneous edit, and on hard ones it may fail even with perfect edit. The filter 𝒞 in Eq. (1) reduces but cannot remove this coupling, motivating second, decoupled signal. Editing Correctness Reward (𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡). To break this coupling, second reward evaluates the edit in isolation, without solving the underlying task. Following the VLM-as-Judge paradigm [43], judge VLM 𝒥 assesses only whether 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 contains the visual information needed to answer 𝑞: 𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞) = 1[𝒥 (𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞) = 1]. (3) 𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡 lifts the capability ceiling: 𝒥 can recognize that bounding box highlights the correct referent or an annotation traces the valid path, even without the reasoning depth to derive the answer, giving broader notion of edit adequacy. The cost is judge noise: 𝒥 may accept plausible-but-uninformative edit, or reject correct one due to superficial visual differences. The two rewards are complementary: 𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒 is faithful but ceiling-bound, while 𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡 is ceiling-lifting but judge-noisy. We combine them as convex sum: ℛ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎) = 𝛼 𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒 + 𝛽 𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡, (4) with 𝛼 = 𝛽 = 0.5 by default, so that each term partially compensates for the others blind spot. To reduce variance, we soften both indicators into empirical probabilities: ℳ and 𝒥 are each queried with 𝐾 stochastic decodings, and 𝑟𝑔𝑢𝑖𝑑𝑒, 𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡 are the fractions of correct answers and positive verdicts, respectively. Optimization. We use Pref-GRPO [32], pairwise-preference extension of GRPO [26]. Given rollout 𝑗=1 from the same (𝑖, 𝑞), we compute pairwise win rate for each image under group of 𝐺 edited images {𝑖𝑗 the combined reward ℛ. The win rate of image 𝑚 is: 𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡}𝐺 𝑤𝑚 = 1 𝐺 𝑛=𝑚 1[ℛ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑚 𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎) > ℛ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑛 𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞, 𝑎)]. Win rates are normalized within the group to give the advantage: ˆ𝐴𝑚 = 𝑤𝑚 mean({𝑤𝑗}𝐺 std({𝑤𝑗}𝐺 𝑗=1) 𝑗=1) . (5) (6) By replacing absolute reward with pairwise preference, Pref-GRPO amplifies reward variance and absorbs reward noise, mitigating the illusory advantage problem and stabilizing training. 3.4. Edit-Verify-Reason Inference Even well-trained editor can produce misleading intermediates. To prevent such errors from contaminating reasoning, ETCHR adds reflective inference procedure that verifies edit reliability before use. The pipeline has three steps: (1) Edit. The editor takes the concatenated prompt [𝑝𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘, 𝑞] with image 𝑖 and produces candidate edit 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡. (2) Verify. ℳ performs binary check on whether 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 contains the visual information needed to answer 𝑞, without producing the answer itself. (3) Reason. If verification passes, ℳ conditions on both 𝑖 and 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 to produce the answer: 𝑎 = ℳ(𝑖, 𝑖𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡, 𝑞). Otherwise, it falls back to the original image alone: 𝑎 = ℳ(𝑖, 𝑞). Verification is vital as edit errors are asymmetric in cost: correct edit gives decisive visual guidance, while an incorrect one introduces structured confounders that MLLMs struggle to override. 6 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Table 1: Main results on five task families. ETCHR is compared against tool-based and programmatic baselines and unified multimodal models. marks tasks outside baselines supported image-editing scope; all numbers are Pass@1 at temperature 0. Models Fine-grained Perception Chart Understanding Logic Jigsaw 3D Understanding Avg. V*Bench HRBench (4K / 8K) ChartQA CharXiv (DQ / RQ) Maze Frozen Lake COCO Jigsaw ViewSpatial Person-RelDir DL3DV-2k DeepEyesV2 [8] Thyme [42] 81.8 81.2 Tool-based & Programmatic Models 0.5 0.0 78.6 / 48.9 66.1 / 45.3 88.4 86.5 77.9 / 73.8 77.1 / 71. 0.0 2.0 6.5 6.5 39.3 39.2 69.0 70.8 51.34 49.69 Bagel-Zebra-CoT [15] ThinkMorph-7B [7] 50.8 65.8 51.9 / 45.8 59.6 / 50.9 71.8 76.4 62.8 / 37.4 61.2 / 28.1 0.0 6.5 1.5 31. 5.5 10.5 28.6 32.2 64.9 62.4 38.27 44.05 Baselines & Our Image Editing Model Unified-Models 9.5 84.8 13.0 86.9 +2.1 +2.5 / +0.8 +1.7 +1.8 / +1.8 +11.0 +4.0 +3.5 78.1 / 74.5 80.6 / 75.3 85.0 / 47.9 86.8 / 49.7 86.7 88.4 27.5 38.5 9.5 13. 69.6 72.3 +2.7 85.9 87.4 +1.5 81.5 / 74.5 82.9 / 75.1 +1.4 / 0.6 85.6 / 80.6 86.8 / 81.1 +1.2 / 0.5 27.5 81.2 83.3 36.0 +2.1 +0.8 / 2.2 +11.5 +6.5 +8.5 93.8 / 68.6 94.6 / 70. 62.5 69.0 40.0 51.5 44.5 93.7 94.1 70.5 +0.4 +0.1 / 0.8 +2.5 +4.5 +26.0 98.3 / 77.1 98.4 / 77.9 95.5 98.0 52.5 57. 41.1 57.2 +16.1 46.6 57.8 +11.2 54.9 57.7 +2.8 70.8 55.95 78.6 60.77 +8.2 +4.82 70.1 65.08 82.7 70.55 +12.6 +5.47 73.4 76.55 83.9 81.16 +10.5 +4. Qwen3-VL-8B [1] +ETCHR Improvement Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite [4] +ETCHR Improvement Kimi K2.5 [29] +ETCHR Improvement 4. Experiments Models. We build our pipeline on top of multiple understanding models: the open-source Qwen3-VL-8BInstruct [1] (dense model with 8B parameters), Kimi K2.5 [29] (Mixture-of-Experts model with 1T parameters), and the closed-source Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite-Preview [4]. Benchmarks. We evaluate our model on five categories of understanding tasks. (1) Fine-grained perception: V*Bench [38], which probes recognition of small details in high-resolution natural images, and HRBench (4K/8K) [31], which extends this setting to 4K and 8K inputs. (2) Chart understanding: ChartQA [22] for question answering over bar, line, and pie charts, and CharXiv [33] (Descriptive/Reasoning), which targets charts from arXiv papers. (3) Logical reasoning: 200 Maze and 200 Frozen Lake tasks that we construct in-house, requiring the model to navigate from start cell to goal while respecting walls or hazards. (4) Jigsaw: 200 puzzles built from MS COCO [17] images by applying tile-shuffling strategies, which probe whether the model can recover image structure from permuted patches. (5) 3D perception and reasoning: the Person-Perspective Relative-Direction subtask of ViewSpatial-Bench [16], together with DL3DV-2k [18], benchmark we constructed using scenes and images from DL3DV, comprising 2000 questions targeting the reasoning capability over 3D perspective-transformation. Implementation Details. We report accuracy (Pass@1, temperature 0) across all experiments. For baselines, we set the maximum number of tool invocations to 10 for structured-action models, and the maximum number of image generations to 10 for unified models. Training Details. For Reasoning Imitation Supervised Fine-tuning, we use Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the Diffusion Transformer of FLUX.2-klein-base-9B for 1 epoch with lora rank 𝑟 = 768, learning rate 𝑙𝑟 = 1𝑒4, Classifier-free guidance 𝑠𝑐𝑎𝑙𝑒 = 1, inference steps 𝑠𝑡𝑒𝑝 = 30. The supervised fine-tuning framework is built on DiffSynth-Studio. For Reasoning Enhancement Reinforcement Learning, we use Pref-GRPO to further fine-tune the DiT. The RL framework is built on Pref-GRPO [32]. We also adapt LoRA fine-tuning strategy, with lora rank 𝑟 = 128, lora alpha 𝛼 = 128, learning rate 𝑙𝑟 = 1𝑒4, Group Number for each rollout 𝐺 = 8. We use Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [1] as both the understanding model ℳ and judge model 𝒥 to provide editing guidance reward and editing correctness reward. 7 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Table 2: Comparison with closed-source image editor. Pass@1 of Qwen3-VL-8B and Gemini-3.1Flash-Lite with Nano Banana 2 (NB2) vs. Ours on 100-sample-per-benchmark subset. Table 3: Ablation of the reflection mechanism. Pass@1 with and without the verification step in the Edit-Verify-Reason pipeline across Perception (Per.), Chart (Cha.), Logic (Log.), Jigsaw (Jig.), and 3D. Model Per. Cha. Log. Jig. 3D Avg. Model Per. Cha. Log. Jig. 3D Avg. Qwen3-VL [1] + NB2 [6] + Ours Gemini-3.1 [4] + NB2 [6] + Ours 80.3 82.0 82.0 75.3 77.7 77. 75.7 77.0 77.3 83.0 84.0 84.3 17.5 20.5 24.0 50.5 53.5 61.0 11.0 13.0 15.0 28.0 32.0 35. 56.0 55.5 66.0 60.0 59.0 68.5 48.10 49.60 52.86 59.36 61.24 65.22 Qwen3-VL [1] w.o. reflection w. reflection Gemini-3.1 [4] w.o. reflection w. reflection 79.1 79.3 80.9 75.2 76.0 76.8 73.2 73.2 75.0 81.2 81.8 82.9 18.5 25.5 26.0 51.3 59.0 60. 9.5 13.0 13.0 27.5 34.5 36.0 56.0 68.2 67.9 58.4 70.7 70.3 47.26 51.84 52.56 58.72 64.40 65. 4.1. Main Results Overall Performance. Tab. 1 compares ETCHR against the two prevailing paradigms. Tool-based and programmatic models are competitive on chart understanding and within few points on fine-grained perception, but their predefined action spaces do not cover tasks requiring non-local edits (Logic, Jigsaw, 3D), which we mark with to indicate unsupported task scope rather than evaluated failure. Unified models avoid this coverage limit by natively interleaving generation, yet on average they trail specialized understanding backbones by more than ten points (e.g., ThinkMorph-7B [7] at 44.05 and Bagel-Zebra-CoT [15] at 38.27 vs. Qwen3-VL-8B [1] at 55.95), reflecting the well-known tension between jointly optimized generative head and pure understanding quality. ETCHR sidesteps both pitfalls by treating specialist editing model as question-conditioned reasoning module, and improves every evaluated backbone (Qwen3-VL-8B [1], Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite [4], Kimi K2.5 [29]) across the task families where comparisons are available. Comparison with Closed-Source Editor. Tab. 2 compares our editor against the closed-source Nano Banana 2 [6] as the visual-thought provider for Qwen3-VL-8B [1] and Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite [4]. To contain API cost, both editors are evaluated on the same 100-sample-per-benchmark subset, so absolute numbers should be read as trends rather than significance-tested estimates. Both editors improve over the no-edit baseline on most tasks, confirming that question-conditioned edits are useful even when produced by generic frontier editor. Our editor is comparable to Nano Banana 2 on Perception and Chart and shows larger margins on Logic, Jigsaw, and 3D, where edits must encode task structure rather than re-render local regions. Results are consistent with our hypothesis that reasoning-aware training, not editor scale, drives guidance quality on structural tasks. 4.2. Ablation Analysis To isolate each design choice in ETCHR, we ablate (i) the two-stage training recipe (Tab. 4), (ii) the two RL reward signals, Editing Correctness and Editing Guidance (Tab. 5), and (iii) the inference-time reflection mechanism (Tab. 3). All settings are evaluated with both understanding backbones, Qwen3-VL-8B [1] and Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite [4]. Training Recipe: Tab. 4 compares the base FLUX.2-klein-base-9B [14] editor with its Stage SFT and Stage II RL checkpoints. The base editor (where evaluable) tracks the no-edit backbone. Stage SFT improves all five task families on both backbones, suggesting the LLM encoder retains the reasoning capacity needed for question-conditioned editing and that supervised imitation suffices to reactivate it. Stage II RL adds under one point on Perception and Chart and is essentially flat on Logic, Jigsaw, and 3D. We attribute this to the sampling granularity of GRPO [26]: Perception and Chart involve localized edits whose group-sampled variants are spatially distinguishable by the reward, whereas Jigsaw and 3D require structural edits, which group sampling appears less able to produce, motivating future work on hierarchical or semantic-aware sampling. Reward Mechanism: Tab. 5 isolates the two RL reward signals introduced in Sec. 3.3: Editing Correctness (isolated edit quality judged by VLM-as-Judge [43]) and Editing Guidance (downstream answer accuracy). Each reward has complementary blind spots, and neither alone matches the full model. Guidance-only is on par with Correctness-only on Logic, where the downstream model benefits directly from edits that expose task-relevant structure, whereas Correctness-only is slightly stronger on Perception and Chart, where the judge can reliably assess localized visual edits such as bounding boxes and chart annotations. Combining the two 8 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Table 4: Ablation of the two-stage training recipe. Pass@1 of the base FLUX.2-klein-base-9B [14] editor, after Stage SFT (+sft), and after Stage II RL (+sft&rl), paired with Qwen3-VL-8B [1] and Gemini-3.1-FlashLite [4]. Table 5: Ablation of the two RL reward signals in Stage II RL. Pass@1 with Editing Correctness only (Cor.), Editing Guidance only (Gui.), or both, , paired with Qwen3-VL-8B. Model Per. Cha. Log. Qwen3-VL [1] 79.1 73. 18.5 Flux [14] + sft + sft&rl 74.3 79.7 80.9 68.9 74.1 75.0 18.5 25.8 26.0 Jig. 9.5 9.5 13.0 13.0 3D Avg. 56.0 47. 55.1 67.3 67.9 45.26 51.98 52.56 Gemini-3.1 [4] 75.2 81.2 51. 27.5 58.4 58.72 Flux [14] + sft + sft&rl 70.3 76.0 76.8 74.5 82.4 82. 51.3 59.5 60.3 27.0 36.0 36.0 59.5 70.0 70.3 56.52 64.78 65.26 Cor. Gui. Per. Cha. Log. Jig. 3D Avg. Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [1] (cid:33) (cid:37) (cid:33) (cid:33) (cid:37) (cid:33) (cid:37) 80.7 (cid:33) 80.2 (cid:33) 80.9 74.7 74.2 75.0 25.8 26.0 26.0 13.0 13.0 13. 66.8 67.6 67.9 52.20 52.20 52.56 Gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview [4] (cid:37) 76.6 (cid:33) 76.1 (cid:33) 76.8 82.5 82.5 82.9 60.0 60.0 60. 35.5 36.0 36.0 69.7 69.9 70.3 64.86 64.90 65.26 Figure 4: Qualitative comparison of ETCHR with existing methods. ETCHR provides the most precise guidance for this problem, assisting the reasoning model in generating the correct answer. matches or exceeds either reward alone across all task families: Correctness provides fidelity floor by filtering implausible edits, while Guidance raises the task ceiling by ensuring each edit advances the reasoning chain. Reflection Mechanism. Tab. 3 ablates the Edit-Verify-Reason pipeline by removing the verification step, so every edited image is passed to the understanding model. Reflection yields consistent gains on Perception and Chart, where the judge can reliably flag mis-localized edits and where backbone accuracy is already high (around 80%); in this regime, suppressing harmful edits matters more than supplying additional hints. Gains are smaller on Logic and absent on Jigsaw for Qwen3-VL-8B [1], with small regression on 3D. Baseline accuracy is low on these tasks. The model rarely solves these tasks from the original image alone, and we hypothesize that even imperfect edits are more useful as hints than discarded. This suggests reflection should be applied selectively, gated by backbone confidence or task family rather than uniformly. Case Analysis. Fig. 4 demonstrates how various think with images strategies assist comprehension through image editing. DeepEyesV2 and Thyme failed to invoke the editing tools, leading to incorrect final answers. Bagel and ThinkMorph performed incorrect edits, also resulting in wrong answers. While Nano Banana 2 executed the edit correctly, the highlighted regions lacked precision, providing minimal guidance for the reasoning model. In contrast, our editing model provides extremely precise edits, enabling the reasoning model to answer the question correctly. 9 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning 5. Related Work Thinking with Images: Tool-based Pipelines. Tool-based think with images methods couple the understanding MLLM with deterministic external renderer: the MLLM emits structured action and the renderer executes it on the input image, producing the intermediate visual aid that feeds the next reasoning step. Visual Sketchpad [10] is an early instance, prompting the MLLM to draw spatial annotations and auxiliary lines for geometric and mathematical reasoning. V* [38] and DeepEyes [44] specialize this idea to fine-grained visual search by emitting bounding-box and crop-zoom commands that progressively localize the queried region, while Thyme [42] and DeepEyes-V2 [8] extend the action space to executable code snippets for chart parsing and multi-step chart understanding. Although the resulting actions are interpretable and reproducible, two limitations persist. First, the action space is fixed at design time, so global, free-form, or task-novel transformations (e.g., redrawing maze path, reordering jigsaw tiles, re-rendering 3D viewpoint) lie outside what the renderer can produce. Second, each new family of actions typically requires task-specific fine-tuning of the understanding model, which can erode its general competence [13] and forces separate adaptation per deployment. ETCHR avoids both costs by replacing the deterministic renderer with question-conditioned image editor whose transformation space is continuous, and by keeping the understanding model frozen so single trained editor plugs into different openand closed-source MLLMs without further fine-tuning. Thinking with Images: Unified Multi-modal Models. second family pursues think with images with single backbone that natively interleaves text and image tokens, removing the external renderer altogether. Chameleon [28] and Show-o [39] demonstrate joint autoregressive and diffusion modeling over shared token space, while Janus [37] decouples the visual encoder for understanding from the one used for generation to relieve the optimization tension between the two objectives. More recently, ThinkMorph [7] and Zebra-CoT [15] fine-tune unified models on interleaved reasoning traces and report emergent adaptive modality switching. Despite this flexibility, the jointly optimized generative head trails specialist image editors in fidelity, so its intermediate images frequently inject noise rather than evidence, as quantified by UniG2U-Bench [35]; the same backbone also lags specialist understanding models on pure perception tasks [37, 39]. ETCHR sidesteps this failure by keeping understanding and generation in separate specialist models: the editor is trained on reasoning-aware objectives without touching the understanding MLLM, and edit fidelity is preserved because the editor is not asked to also serve as the answer producer. Image Editing Models. Recent image-to-image editing methods such as FLUX-class models [14] and QwenImage-Edit [36] replace shallow CLIP-style text encoders with MLLM-style encoders, expanding the editors capacity to parse complex instructions. Closest in spirit are instruction-tuned diffusion editors: InstructPix2Pix [2] fine-tunes latent diffusion model on synthetic before/after pairs, and MagicBrush [41] improves edit faithfulness with human-annotated real-image edits. Across all of these, however, the training objective remains anchored in aesthetic fidelity and instruction-following accuracy: the user is assumed to supply an explicit edit prompt, so the editor is optimized as faithful instruction renderer and is never asked to infer what edit would help answer an abstract question. ETCHR targets this gap by repurposing the MLLM-encoder editor as question-conditioned, reasoning-aware module rather than passive instruction-following renderer. 6. Conclusion We present ETCHR, question-conditioned image editor for think with images reasoning. Our analysis identifies two gaps in previous works, language-side gap in mapping abstract questions to edits and generation-side gap under increasing reasoning depth, and motivates two-stage recipe (Reasoning Imitation SFT and Reasoning Enhancement RL) paired with an Edit-Verify-Reason inference procedure. Decoupling the editor from the understanding model lets ETCHR plug into openand closed-source MLLMs in training-free manner. Limitations: GRPO sampling granularity: GRPOs sampling exhibits limited semantic diversity for structural editing (e.g., jigsaw restoration), which constrains policy exploration and prevents uniform RL gains across all reasoning families. Downstream capability bound: ETCHR end-to-end performance remains capped by the understanding model; even optimally edited intermediates cannot overcome the reasoning ceiling of the downstream MLLM. Additional time cost: Image editing incurs higher temporal cost compared to text-based reasoning; consequently, ETCHR increases the time overhead for samples requiring only short CoT. 10 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning

Advances in neural information processing systems, 36:4659546623, 2023. 3.3, 4.2 [44] Ziwei Zheng, Michael Yang, Jack Hong, Chenxiao Zhao, Guohai Xu, Le Yang, Chao Shen, and Xing arXiv preprint Incentivizing" thinking with images" via reinforcement learning. Yu. Deepeyes: arXiv:2505.14362, 2025. 1, 5 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning A. Prompts Task-level Prompt: Fine-grained Perception: Draw red box to mark the important regions for this question in the figure. Chart Understanding: Draw red box to mark the important regions for this question in the figure. Logic: Draw the shortest path of the maze in blue. Jigsaw: Draw the original image after restoring this jigsaw puzzle task. 3D Understanding: Imagine new perspective of the original image that helps answer the question. Verify Prompt: Fine-grained Perception: Please judge whether the information relevant to solving this problem is marked with red box in the picture. If the red box contains valid information, reply with 1; if there is no red box in the picture, or the area enclosed by the red box does not contain valid information, reply with 0. Chart Understanding: Please judge whether the information relevant to solving this problem is marked with red box in the picture. If the red box contains valid information, reply with 1; if there is no red box in the picture, or the area enclosed by the red box does not contain valid information, reply with 0. Maze: Please determine whether the following image contains valid maze path: the middle path (blue) connects the starting point (green) to the destination (red), is continuous in four directions (up, down, left, right) with no diagonal movement allowed, and no black obstacles or white path tiles have been altered compared to Figure 1 (the original maze) in Figure 2 (the path diagram). If the maze if valid, reply with 1. Otherwise, return with 0. Frozen Lake: Please determine whether the path drawn with red lines in Figure 2 connects the treasure to the character without passing through any holes. If the frozen lake if valid, reply with 1. Otherwise, reply with 0. Jigsaw: Please determine whether Figure 2 is correct restoration of the jigsaw puzzle task in Figure 1. If Fig.2 is correct, reply with 1. Otherwise, reply with 0. 3D Understanding: judge: Please judge whether you can see the objects mentioned in the question from the perspective of the second image. If the objects are presented in the image, reply with 1. Otherwise, reply with 0. Reason Prompt: Fine-grained Perception: Answer the question based on the two images above. The first image is the original one corresponding to the question, while the second one uses red box to mark the area containing the key information for the question in the image. Please first focus on the boxed part in the second image, and answer the question based on the corresponding information in the two images. Chart Understanding: Answer the question based on the two images above. The first image is the original one corresponding to the question, while the second one uses red box to mark the area containing the key information for the question in the image. Please focus on the second image to answer the question. 14 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 5: ETCHR enables precise localization of granular information in charts, facilitating better comprehension of the visual data for understanding models. B. More Cases We further provide examples of ETCHR assisting models in performing reasoning across tasks such as chart understanding  (Fig. 5)  , logical reasoning  (Fig. 6)  , jigsaw puzzles, and 3D perception  (Fig. 8)  . 15 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 6: ETCHR can draw the correct path on the maze map, helping understanding model accurately identify the route to exit the maze. Figure 7: ETCHR is capable of restoring scrambled jigsaw images, thereby guiding the understanding model to correctly solve jigsaw tasks. 16 ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning Figure 8: ETCHR enables perspective shifting based on the specific question, helping to ensure the model correctly understands spatial positioning.
